ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a spinach food product entry; stir-fried preparation variant is not separately coded. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers cooked spinach; stir-fried with oil and aromatics is the closest defensible match within the cooked spinach category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for stir-fried spinach dish; left null to avoid fabrication.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical side dish serving of stir-fried spinach is approximately 100 g, equivalent to a small bowl or side plate portion as commonly served in Singaporean hawker centres and home cooking.

nutrient_source.title: Spinach, stir-fried — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo entry for stir-fried spinach (F14080057). All core macros and secondary nutrients are sourced from this entry with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of non-starchy vegetables — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for cooked leafy green vegetables
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for stir-fried spinach in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate based on published GI values for cooked non-starchy leafy green vegetables (GI range 10–20, low category) from the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per 100 g serving calculated as (15 × 0.95 g carbohydrate) / 100 = 0.14, rounded to 0.1. This is consistent with spinach being a very low-carbohydrate vegetable with negligible glycaemic impact.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium (195 mg/100 g) with high potassium (~380 mg/100 g) yielding a favourable Na:K ratio consistent with DASH dietary guidelines
hypertension.guidance: Stir-fried spinach is suitable for hypertensive patients due to its low sodium content and high potassium, which supports blood pressure regulation; however, patients should avoid adding extra salt during cooking to preserve this benefit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate (0.95 g/100 g), negligible sugar (0.15 g), low GI (~15), and dietary fibre (1.87 g/100 g) minimise postprandial glycaemic response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Stir-fried spinach is well-suited for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes as its very low carbohydrate content and low glycaemic index result in minimal blood glucose elevation, making it an appropriate regular vegetable side dish within a diabetes-friendly meal plan.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (~1.1 g/100 g), no trans fat, high potassium, and nitrate-rich spinach supporting endothelial function
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Stir-fried spinach is appropriate for cardiovascular disease management due to its low saturated fat, absence of trans fat, and the vasodilatory properties of dietary nitrates in spinach; using unsaturated cooking oil (e.g., canola or olive oil) further enhances its cardiovascular benefit.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Antioxidant-rich spinach (vitamin C, vitamin E, iron) and anti-inflammatory aromatics (garlic) with low saturated fat and no trans fat
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Stir-fried spinach is generally suitable for rheumatoid arthritis patients due to its antioxidant content and anti-inflammatory properties; however, patients on certain medications should monitor oxalate intake as spinach is a high-oxalate food.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stir-fried spinach is a simple, nutritious vegetable side dish prepared by quickly cooking fresh spinach leaves in hot oil with aromatics such as garlic, and seasoned lightly with salt. The high-heat stir-frying technique preserves the vibrant green colour and produces a tender yet slightly wilted texture with a savoury, mildly garlicky flavour. It is a staple side dish in Singaporean home cooking and hawker centres, commonly served alongside rice or noodle dishes. Nutritionally, stir-fried spinach is low in carbohydrates (0.95 g/100 g) and calories (81 kcal/100 g), with moderate fat from cooking oil (7.65 g/100 g) and a modest protein content (1.62 g/100 g). Dietary fibre is 1.87 g/100 g and sodium is 195 mg/100 g. The dish has a low glycaemic index (~15) and negligible glycaemic load, making it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, and cardiovascular disease. Spinach is naturally rich in potassium (~380 mg/100 g), dietary nitrates, vitamin C, vitamin E, and iron, supporting blood pressure regulation, endothelial function, and antioxidant defence. The dish is vegetarian, vegan, gluten-free, and dairy-free.

confidence_reason: Core macros and secondary nutrients sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo (F14080057) with confidence 0.95. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database for cooked non-starchy leafy greens, clearly documented. Ontology codes are defensible approximate matches. All multilingual names are verified.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for stir-fried spinach is not separately reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry F14080057. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in HPB Foodo for this entry. calcium_mg, iron_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: micronutrient detail not available in the HPB Foodo entry for this specific stir-fried preparation; USDA FoodData Central (raw spinach) provides indicative micronutrient values but these are not directly transferable to the stir-fried preparation without confirmed adaptation factors. potassium_mg value of 380 mg/100 g is an indicative estimate based on USDA FoodData Central cooked spinach data (SR Legacy) used to support health context narrative only; it is not carried as a primary nutrient claim. saturated_fat_g of 1.1 g/100 g is estimated from USDA FoodData Central cooked spinach with added oil, consistent with the HPB fat total of 7.65 g/100 g; documented as an estimate. trans_fat_g set to 0.0: confirmed negligible/absent for vegetable oil stir-fry preparation. cholesterol_mg set to 0.0: plant-based dish with no animal-derived fat; confirmed zero. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate for cooked non-starchy leafy green vegetables from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct stir-fried spinach GI entry exists in the database as of 2026-04-16. The input record listed 'keto' as a dietary tag; this is retained as the carbohydrate content (0.95 g/100 g) is consistent with ketogenic dietary thresholds.
